m	Z	m
d d!
d!e"
d"d#
d$d%
d%e%
Additional handlers for the logging package for Python. The core package is
based on PEP 282 and comments thereto in comp.lang.python.
Copyright (C) 2001-2016 Vinay Sajip. All Rights Reserved.
To use, simply 'import logging.handlers' and log away!
ST_DEV
ST_INO
ST_MTIMEi<#
BaseRotatingHandlerz
    Base class for handlers that rotate log files at a certain point.
    Not meant to be instantiated directly.  Instead, use RotatingFileHandler
    or TimedRotatingFileHandler.
    NFc
        Use the specified filename for streamed logging
        
mode
encoding
delay
errorsN)
logging

self

(/usr/lib64/python3.9/logging/handlers.pyr
BaseRotatingHandler.__init__c
        Emit a record.
        Output the record to the file, catering for rollover as described
        in doRollover().
        N)
shouldRollover
doRolloverr
emit
	Exception
handleError
recordr
BaseRotatingHandler.emitc
        Modify the filename of a log file when rotating.
        This is provided so that a custom filename can be provided.
        The default implementation calls the 'namer' attribute of the
        handler, if it's callable, passing the default name to
        it. If the attribute isn't callable (the default is None), the name
        is returned unchanged.
        :param default_name: The default name for the log file.
        )
callable
namer)
default_name
resultr
rotation_filenameO
z%BaseRotatingHandler.rotation_filenamec
        When rotating, rotate the current log.
        The default implementation calls the 'rotator' attribute of the
        handler, if it's callable, passing the source and dest arguments to
        it. If the attribute isn't callable (the default is None), the source
        is simply renamed to the destination.
        :param source: The source filename. This is normally the base
                       filename, e.g. 'test.log'
        :param dest:   The destination filename. This is normally
                       what the source is rotated to, e.g. 'test.log.1'.
        N)
rotator
path
exists
rename)
source
destr
rotateb
BaseRotatingHandler.rotate)
NFN)
__name__
__module__
__qualname__
__doc__r
RotatingFileHandlerz
    Handler for logging to a set of files, which switches from one file
    to the next when the current file reaches a certain size.
    
        Open the specified file and use it as the stream for logging.
        By default, the file grows indefinitely. You can specify particular
        values of maxBytes and backupCount to allow the file to rollover at
        a predetermined size.
        Rollover occurs whenever the current log file is nearly maxBytes in
        length. If backupCount is >= 1, the system will successively create
        new files with the same pathname as the base file, but with extensions
        ".1", ".2" etc. appended to it. For example, with a backupCount of 5
        and a base file name of "app.log", you would get "app.log",
        "app.log.1", "app.log.2", ... through to "app.log.5". The file being
        written to is always "app.log" - when it gets filled up, it is closed
        and renamed to "app.log.1", and if files "app.log.1", "app.log.2" etc.
        exist, then they are renamed to "app.log.2", "app.log.3" etc.
        respectively.
        If maxBytes is zero, rollover never occurs.
        r
maxBytes
backupCount)
RotatingFileHandler.__init__c
        Do a rollover, as described in __init__().
        Nr
%s.%dz
stream
closer/
ranger
baseFilenamer 
remover#
_open)
dfnr
RotatingFileHandler.doRolloverc
        Determine if rollover should occur.
        Basically, see if the supplied record would cause the file to exceed
        the size limit we have.
        FNr
isfiler2
format
seek
tell
msgr
z"RotatingFileHandler.shouldRollover)
NFN)
TimedRotatingFileHandlerz
    Handler for logging to a file, rotating the log file at certain timed
    intervals.
    If backupCount is > 0, when rollover is done, no more than backupCount
    files are kept - the oldest ones are deleted.
    
%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%Sz-^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}-\d{2}-\d{2}(\.\w+)?$
%Y-%m-%d_%H-%Mz'^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}-\d{2}(\.\w+)?$
%Y-%m-%d_%Hz!^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}_\d{2}(\.\w+)?$
MIDNIGHTr
%Y-%m-%dz
^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}(\.\w+)?$
zHYou must specify a day for weekly rollover from 0 to 6 (0 is Monday): %s
6z-Invalid day specified for weekly rollover: %sz'Invalid rollover interval specified: %s)
upper

        record is not used, as we are just comparing times, but it is needed so
        the method signatures are the same

        Determine the files to delete when rolling over.

)	ax
        do a rollover; in this case, a date/time stamp is appended to the filename
        when the rollover happens.  However, you want the file to be named for the
        start of the interval, not the current time.  If there is a backup count,
        then we have to get a list of matching filenames, sort them and remove
        the one with the oldest suffix.
        Nr1
strftimerS
Z	timeTupleZ
dstThenrp
z#TimedRotatingFileHandler.doRollover)
NFFNN)	r'
WatchedFileHandlera
    A handler for logging to a file, which watches the file
    to see if it has changed while in use. This can happen because of
    usage of programs such as newsyslog and logrotate which perform
    log file rotation. This handler, intended for use under Unix,
    watches the file to see if it has changed since the last emit.
    (A file has changed if its device or inode have changed.)
    If it has changed, the old file stream is closed, and the file
    opened to get a new stream.
    This handler is not appropriate for use under Windows, because
    under Windows open files cannot be moved or renamed - logging
    opens the files with exclusive locks - and so there is no need
    for such a handler. Furthermore, ST_INO is not supported under
    Windows; stat always returns zero for this value.
    This handler is based on a suggestion and patch by Chad J.
    Schroeder.
    r,
_statstreamr
WatchedFileHandler.__init__c
fstat
filenor
sresr
WatchedFileHandler._statstreamc
        Reopen log file if needed.
        Checks if the underlying file has changed, and if it
        has, close the old stream and reopen the file to get the
        current stream.
        N)
FileNotFoundErrorr
flushr3
reopenIfNeeded
z!WatchedFileHandler.reopenIfNeededc
        Emit a record.
        If underlying file has changed, reopen the file before emitting the
        record to it.
        N)
WatchedFileHandler.emit)
NFN)
SocketHandlera
    A handler class which writes logging records, in pickle format, to
    a streaming socket. The socket is kept open across logging calls.
    If the peer resets it, an attempt is made to reconnect on the next call.
    The pickle which is sent is that of the LogRecord's attribute dictionary
    (__dict__), so that the receiver does not need to have the logging module
    installed in order to process the logging event.
    To unpickle the record at the receiving end into a LogRecord, use the
    makeLogRecord function.
    c
        Initializes the handler with a specific host address and port.
        When the attribute *closeOnError* is set to True - if a socket error
        occurs, the socket is silently closed and then reopened on the next
        logging call.
        NFg
Handlerr
host
port
address
sock
closeOnError
	retryTime
retryStart
retryMax
retryFactor
SocketHandler.__init__r0
        A factory method which allows subclasses to define the precise
        type of socket they want.
        N
timeout)
socketZ
create_connectionr
AF_UNIX
SOCK_STREAMZ
settimeout
connect
OSErrorr3
makeSocket!
SocketHandler.makeSocketc
        Try to create a socket, using an exponential backoff with
        a max retry time. Thanks to Robert Olson for the original patch
        (SF #815911) which has been slightly refactored.
        NT)	r]
retryPeriodr
nowZ
attemptr
createSocket2
SocketHandler.createSocketc
        Send a pickled string to the socket.
        This function allows for partial sends which can happen when the
        network is busy.
        N)
sendallr
sendN
SocketHandler.sendc
        Pickles the record in binary format with a length prefix, and
        returns it ready for transmission across the socket.
        rA

makePicklea
SocketHandler.makePicklec
        Handle an error during logging.
        An error has occurred during logging. Most likely cause -
        connection lost. Close the socket so that we can retry on the
        next event.
        N)
SocketHandler.handleErrorc
        Emit a record.
        Pickles the record and writes it to the socket in binary format.
        If there is an error with the socket, silently drop the packet.
        If there was a problem with the socket, re-establishes the
        socket.
        N)
SocketHandler.emitc
        Closes the socket.
        N)
acquirer
release)
SocketHandler.closeN)
DatagramHandlera
    A handler class which writes logging records, in pickle format, to
    a datagram socket.  The pickle which is sent is that of the LogRecord's
    attribute dictionary (__dict__), so that the receiver does not need to
    have the logging module installed in order to process the logging event.
    To unpickle the record at the receiving end into a LogRecord, use the
    makeLogRecord function.
    c
        Initializes the handler with a specific host address and port.
        FN)
DatagramHandler.__init__c
        The factory method of SocketHandler is here overridden to create
        a UDP socket (SOCK_DGRAM).
        N)
AF_INET
SOCK_DGRAM)
familyr~
DatagramHandler.makeSocketc
        Send a pickled string to a socket.
        This function no longer allows for partial sends which can happen
        when the network is busy - UDP does not guarantee delivery and
        can deliver packets out of sequence.
        N)
sendtor
DatagramHandler.sendN)

SysLogHandlera
    A handler class which sends formatted logging records to a syslog
    server. Based on Sam Rushing's syslog module:
    http://www.nightmare.com/squirl/python-ext/misc/syslog.py
    Contributed by Nicolas Untz (after which minor refactoring changes
    have been made).
    r
alertZ
crit
critical
debugZ
emerg
error
infoZ
noticeZ
panic
warn
warning)
authZ
authprivZ
consoleZ
cron
daemonZ
ftpZ
kernZ
lprZ
mailZ
newsZ
ntpZ
securityz
solaris-cronZ
syslog
userZ
uucpZ
local0Z
local1Z
local2Z
local3Z
local4Z
local5Z
local6Z
local7r
DEBUG
INFO
WARNING
ERROR
CRITICALZ	localhostNc
        Initialize a handler.
        If address is specified as a string, a UNIX socket is used. To log to a
        local syslogd, "SysLogHandler(address="/dev/log")" can be used.
        If facility is not specified, LOG_USER is used. If socktype is
        specified as socket.SOCK_DGRAM or socket.SOCK_STREAM, that specific
        socket type will be used. For Unix sockets, you can also specify a
        socktype of None, in which case socket.SOCK_DGRAM will be used, falling
        back to socket.SOCK_STREAM.
        TFNr
z!getaddrinfo returns an empty list)

z!SysLogHandler._connect_unixsocketc
        Encode the facility and priority. You can pass in strings or
        integers - if strings are passed, the facility_names and
        priority_names mapping dictionaries are used to convert them to
        integers.
        ra
facility_names
priority_names)
priorityr
encodePriority
SysLogHandler.encodePriorityc
SysLogHandler.closec
        Map a logging level name to a key in the priority_names map.
        This is useful in two scenarios: when custom levels are being
        used, and in the case where you can't do a straightforward
        mapping by lowercasing the logging level name because of locale-
        specific issues (see SF #1524081).
        r
priority_map
get)

        Emit a record.
        The record is formatted, and then sent to the syslog server. If
        exception information is present, it is NOT sent to the server.

SMTPHandlerzK
    A handler class which sends an SMTP email for each logging event.
    N
        Initialize the handler.
        Initialize the instance with the from and to addresses and subject
        line of the email. To specify a non-standard SMTP port, use the
        (host, port) tuple format for the mailhost argument. To specify
        authentication credentials, supply a (username, password) tuple
        for the credentials argument. To specify the use of a secure
        protocol (TLS), pass in a tuple for the secure argument. This will
        only be used when authentication credentials are supplied. The tuple
        will be either an empty tuple, or a single-value tuple with the name
        of a keyfile, or a 2-value tuple with the names of the keyfile and
        certificate file. (This tuple is passed to the `starttls` method).
        A timeout in seconds can be specified for the SMTP connection (the
        default is one second).
        N)
list
tuple
mailhost
mailport
username
password
fromaddrr
toaddrs
subject
securer
credentialsr
SMTPHandler.__init__c
        Determine the subject for the email.
        If you want to specify a subject line which is record-dependent,
        override this method.
        )
getSubject
SMTPHandler.getSubjectc
        Emit a record.
        Format the record and send it to the specified addressees.
        r
EmailMessager
From

Date)

utilsrh

ehloZ

smtprA
SMTPHandler.emit)
NTEventLogHandlera
    A handler class which sends events to the NT Event Log. Adds a
    registry entry for the specified application name. If no dllname is
    provided, win32service.pyd (which contains some basic message
    placeholders) is used. Note that use of these placeholders will make
    your event logs big, as the entire message source is held in the log.
    If you want slimmer logs, you have to pass in the name of your own DLL
    which contains the message definitions you want to use in the event log.
    N
Applicationc
win32service.pydzWThe Python Win32 extensions for NT (service, event logging) appear not to be available.)
win32evtlogutil
win32evtlog
appname
_welur 
__file__rw
dllname
logtypeZ
AddSourceToRegistryZ
EVENTLOG_ERROR_TYPE
deftyper
EVENTLOG_INFORMATION_TYPEr
EVENTLOG_WARNING_TYPEr
typemap
ImportError
print)
NTEventLogHandler.__init__c
        Return the message ID for the event record. If you are using your
        own messages, you could do this by having the msg passed to the
        logger being an ID rather than a formatting string. Then, in here,
        you could use a dictionary lookup to get the message ID. This
        version returns 1, which is the base message ID in win32service.pyd.
        r0
getMessageIDE
NTEventLogHandler.getMessageIDc
        Return the event category for the record.
        Override this if you want to specify your own categories. This version
        returns 0.
        r
getEventCategoryO
z"NTEventLogHandler.getEventCategoryc
        Return the event type for the record.
        Override this if you want to specify your own types. This version does
        a mapping using the handler's typemap attribute, which is set up in
        __init__() to a dictionary which contains mappings for DEBUG, INFO,
        WARNING, ERROR and CRITICAL. If you are using your own levels you will
        either need to override this method or place a suitable dictionary in
        the handler's typemap attribute.
        )
levelnor
getEventTypeX
NTEventLogHandler.getEventTypec
rhzD|
        Emit a record.
        Determine the message ID, event category and event type. Then
        log the message in the NT event log.
        N)	r
ReportEventr
typerA
NTEventLogHandler.emitc
        Clean up this handler.
        You can remove the application name from the registry as a
        source of event log entries. However, if you do this, you will
        not be able to see the events as you intended in the Event Log
        Viewer - it needs to be able to access the registry to get the
        DLL name.
        N)
NTEventLogHandler.close)
HTTPHandlerz^
    A class which sends records to a Web server, using either GET or
    POST semantics.
    
GETFNc
        Initialize the instance with the host, the request URL, and the method
        ("GET" or "POST")
        )

        that is sent as the CGI data. Overwrite in your class.

BufferingHandlerz
  A handler class which buffers logging records in memory. Whenever each
  record is added to the buffer, a check is made to see if the buffer should
  be flushed. If it should, then flush() is expected to do what's needed.
    c
        Initialize the handler with the buffer size.
        N)
capacity
buffer)
BufferingHandler.__init__c
        Should the handler flush its buffer?
        Returns true if the buffer is up to capacity. This method can be
        overridden to implement custom flushing strategies.
        )
shouldFlush
BufferingHandler.shouldFlushc
        Emit a record.
        Append the record. If shouldFlush() tells us to, call flush() to process
        the buffer.
        N)
BufferingHandler.emitc
        Override to implement custom flushing behaviour.
        This version just zaps the buffer to empty.
        N)
clearr
BufferingHandler.flushc
        Close the handler.
        This version just flushes and chains to the parent class' close().
        N)
BufferingHandler.closeN)	r'
MemoryHandlerz
    A handler class which buffers logging records in memory, periodically
    flushing them to a target handler. Flushing occurs whenever the buffer
    is full, or when an event of a certain severity or greater is seen.
    NTc
        Initialize the handler with the buffer size, the level at which
        flushing should occur and an optional target.
        Note that without a target being set either here or via setTarget(),
        a MemoryHandler is no use to anyone!
        The ``flushOnClose`` argument is ``True`` for backward compatibility
        reasons - the old behaviour is that when the handler is closed, the
        buffer is flushed, even if the flush level hasn't been exceeded nor the
        capacity exceeded. To prevent this, set ``flushOnClose`` to ``False``.
        N)
flushLevel
target
flushOnClose)
MemoryHandler.__init__c
        Check for buffer full or a record at the flushLevel or higher.
        )
MemoryHandler.shouldFlushc
        Set the target handler for this handler.
        N)
	setTarget7
MemoryHandler.setTargetc
        For a MemoryHandler, flushing means just sending the buffered
        records to the target, if there is one. Override if you want
        different behaviour.
        The record buffer is also cleared by this operation.
        N)
handler'
MemoryHandler.flushc
        Flush, if appropriately configured, set the target to None and lose the
        buffer.
        N)
MemoryHandler.close)
QueueHandlera
    This handler sends events to a queue. Typically, it would be used together
    with a multiprocessing Queue to centralise logging to file in one process
    (in a multi-process application), so as to avoid file write contention
    between processes.
    This code is new in Python 3.2, but this class can be copy pasted into
    user code for use with earlier Python versions.
    c
        Initialise an instance, using the passed queue.
        N)
queue)
QueueHandler.__init__c
        Enqueue a record.
        The base implementation uses put_nowait. You may want to override
        this method if you want to use blocking, timeouts or custom queue
        implementations.
        N)
put_nowaitr
enqueueu
QueueHandler.enqueuec
        Prepares a record for queuing. The object returned by this method is
        enqueued.
        The base implementation formats the record to merge the message
        and arguments, and removes unpickleable items from the record
        in-place.
        You might want to override this method if you want to convert
        the record to a dict or JSON string, or send a modified copy
        of the record while leaving the original intact.
        N)
copyr
exc_textr@
prepare
QueueHandler.preparec
        Emit a record.
        Writes the LogRecord to the queue, preparing it for pickling first.
        N)
QueueHandler.emitN)
QueueListenerz
    This class implements an internal threaded listener which watches for
    LogRecords being added to a queue, removes them and passes them to a
    list of handlers for processing.
    NF)
respect_handler_levelc
        Initialise an instance with the specified queue and
        handlers.
        N)
handlers
_threadr5
QueueListener.__init__c
        Dequeue a record and return it, optionally blocking.
        The base implementation uses get. You may want to override this method
        if you want to use timeouts or work with custom queue implementations.
        )
blockr
dequeue
QueueListener.dequeuec
        Start the listener.
        This starts up a background thread to monitor the queue for
        LogRecords to process.
        )
	threadingZ
Thread
_monitorr7
start)
QueueListener.startc
        Prepare a record for handling.
        This method just returns the passed-in record. You may want to
        override this method if you need to do any custom marshalling or
        manipulation of the record before passing it to the handlers.
        r
QueueListener.preparec
        Handle a record.
        This just loops through the handlers offering them the record
        to handle.
        TN)
levelr-
handlerZ
processr
QueueListener.handlec
        Monitor the queue for records, and ask the handler
        to deal with them.
        This method runs on a separate, internal thread.
        The thread will terminate if it sees a sentinel object in the queue.
        
	task_doneTN)
hasattrr9
	_sentinelr>
Empty)
has_task_doner
QueueListener._monitorc
        This is used to enqueue the sentinel record.
        The base implementation uses put_nowait. You may want to override this
        method if you want to use timeouts or work with custom queue
        implementations.
        N)
enqueue_sentinel
QueueListener.enqueue_sentinelc
        Stop the listener.
        This asks the thread to terminate, and then waits for it to do so.
        Note that if you don't call this before your application exits, there
        may be some records still left on the queue, which won't be processed.
        N)
stop	
QueueListener.stop)
)'r*
DEFAULT_TCP_LOGGING_PORTZ

DEFAULT_HTTP_LOGGING_PORTZ

SYSLOG_TCP_PORTri
objectr4
<module>
